text: "A lever applied from outside? But we are sixty feet above the ground. How would anyone -" "That is precisely the question which makes these marks so interesting," replied Ashworth. "I do not yet have a complete answer. But consider this." He pointed upward to where the battlements rose above, and then outward to where the main roof of the Hall extended some fifteen feet distant. "The roof line is lower than the gallery parapet by approximately eight feet. From a particular angle, given the right equipment and a knowledge of the building's geometry, it might not be entirely impossible to apply mechanical force to this point from a position on the roof below." "You are suggesting that someone engineered this fall - that Edmund Blackwood was thrown from this parapet?" "I am suggesting that the physical evidence is not inconsistent with such a hypothesis, and that several features of the scene are very difficult to account for on the hypothesis of accident. I am not yet in a position to do more than suggest." He straightened and looked about him once more with that comprehensive, cataloguing regard. "There is one further observation. Come here." I followed him to the door through which we had emerged onto the gallery, and he directed my attention to the lock. It was a simple iron bolt, operated from the gallery side only - the door could be locked from without, but not from within. In the bolt mechanism itself, in the crevice where the bolt slid into its housing, there was a fragment of material - a few threads of dark grey wool.

Francis Ardley, brought into the library and seated in a chair from which escape was impossible - Sir Percival and I blocking the door, and Ashworth standing before him with his characteristic air of patient, comprehensive intelligence - presented an aspect of studied contempt that I found, in its way, more impressive than fear would have been. "Well," he said, in a voice entirely under his control, "it appears you have been more thorough than I estimated." "You have been returning to Edmund's room on multiple occasions since his death," said Ashworth, "in search of documentary evidence which he had assembled against the Selkirk Company. On your first visit, you found and removed the portfolio from the hidden compartment - you knew of the compartment, I surmise, either because Edmund had shown it to you in the past, or because you had made your own examination of the desk on a previous occasion. But you have continued to search because you believe there are additional documents - the photographs, perhaps, which I have already secured, or other materials which you have not yet located." A silence. Then: "You are rather better informed than I would have expected." "The ghost of Edmund Blackwood," continued Ashworth. "A performance - part of the same general effort to recover the evidence and simultaneously to create a climate of supernatural disturbance which would discourage rational investigation. It served a double purpose: it allowed you to enter the house at night under cover, and it ensured that any reports from servants or family members would be treated with scepticism by official investigators." "An ingenious scheme," said Ardley, and something in his voice suggested he meant it as a comment on his own ingenuity. "Ingenious," agreed Ashworth. "But you have made several mistakes, Mr. Ardley. The principal mistake was made three weeks ago, when you killed Edmund Blackwood." The silence that followed was of a different, heavier quality. "I should be very careful," said Ardley quietly, "about making that particular accusation without evidence to support it." "I have evidence enough to begin with. The marks on the turret parapet - consistent with a mechanical device applied from the roof. The wool caught in the door bolt - consistent with a specific coat which I expect to find in your rooms. The programme of your movements on the evening of Edmund's death, which I have now been able to reconstruct from statements you believed too casual to be remembered. And the testimony of a witness whom I have not yet named, but who observed you on the roof of the Hall at a time which places you in proximity to the turret at the relevant hour." The colour had left Ardley's face entirely. "I shall require a solicitor," he said at last. "An admirable and entirely appropriate decision," said Ashworth. "Sir Percival, I believe it is time to send for the police."

Whatever he was involved in, he was not a reckless man. He had calculated his responses to Ashworth's questions with considerable speed and skill, and his recovery from the moment of genuine surprise - at the mention of the Selkirk Mining Company - had been swift. Ashworth, for his part, spent the first part of the evening at his writing desk, composing a series of telegrams which he dispatched that night by the messenger service he habitually employed for urgent communications. By the following morning, several replies had arrived, and he spent the breakfast hour in their perusal, his face alight with that particular satisfaction which the confirmation of a hypothesis produces in a mind constituted like his. "The man in the photograph," he announced, setting down the last of the telegrams and reaching for his coffee, "is Mr. Vincent Calder. He is a financier of the second rank, known in commercial circles chiefly for a series of speculative mining ventures in Canada and Australia. Three of these ventures have produced extraordinary returns for their promoters and catastrophic losses for their investors. The Selkirk Mining Company is his most recent enterprise, and is presently in the process of raising capital through a prospectus which represents, according to my informant in the City, the most carefully constructed piece of financial fiction since the South Sea Bubble." "A fraudulent company?" "A company whose principal asset - the mineral rights to several hundred thousand acres of British Columbian territory - has been systematically misrepresented to prospective investors. The geological surveys which underpin the prospectus were conducted by a geologist who has since been discredited in connection with two previous Calder ventures, and whose reports were, it appears, written to specification rather than from observation."

"You think he was deceived into going up there," said Miss Helena, and something in her voice indicated that the manner of her uncle's death had been, in its particular cruelty, a source of especial distress. "I think he was deceived, yes. And I think the deception was carefully planned and required both a knowledge of Edmund's movements and an understanding of his character - his honesty, his thoroughness, his determination to see the right thing done. Ardley exploited those qualities. It is, in the final analysis, one of the most contemptible features of the whole affair." Miss Helena was silent for a moment, and we walked on through the frost-bright park. "He succeeded in one thing, at least," she said at last. "Uncle Edmund's death will ensure that the Selkirk fraud is fully exposed. Every investor who was deceived will now have recourse in law. That is what he was working toward." "It is," said Ashworth. "And it is no small thing." She stopped, and looked at him with that direct, luminous gaze. "Thank you," she said. "For everything you have done here. Not only for establishing the truth of what happened - though that matters enormously - but for the manner in which you have done it. You have treated this family's grief and this family's shame with more consideration than we had any right to expect from a professional inquiry." Ashworth received this with a slight formal inclination of the head which conveyed appreciation without embarrassment. "It is the kind of case," he said, "which makes this work worthwhile. When the pursuit of truth serves simultaneously the claims of justice and the needs of those who loved the person at the centre of it, one is reminded of why the work matters." He looked across the frost-bright park toward the grey bulk of the Hall. "Come, let us go in. I believe Sir Percival's meeting will have concluded, and there are one or two practical matters still to arrange."

"Someone forced this door," said Ashworth quietly. "The wool is caught at a point consistent with the fabric of a coat being pressed hard against the bolt while the door was being held shut from the outside. The implication is that Edmund Blackwood may have been prevented from retreating from the gallery - held out here against his will." The wind moved about us with renewed force, and I confess that standing upon that bleak and elevated gallery, contemplating the evidence of a man's last moments, I felt a coldness that had nothing to do with the November temperature. "This is murder," I said flatly. "This is the evidence of human agency applied to this place and to this circumstance," said Ashworth, with his habitual care for precision. "Whether it constitutes murder, and by whom that murder was committed, are matters yet to be established. Let us proceed to the late Mr. Edmund Blackwood's room." Edmund Blackwood's bedroom was a well-appointed chamber on the first floor of the main house, furnished with the solid, comfortable elegance of the previous generation's taste - heavy mahogany pieces, good Turkey carpet, thick curtains of dark green wool at the windows. A writing desk stood near the window, and upon it, arranged in the careful order which Merton had evidently maintained since the room was sealed, were the dead man's papers. Ashworth went directly to the desk and began a systematic examination of its contents. There were several letters - social correspondence of no apparent significance, a letter from a firm of stockbrokers regarding certain investments, and a draft of an unfinished letter addressed to a solicitor by the name of Hartley, of Lincoln's Inn, which Ashworth read twice with evident interest.

He paused. "But you are right that there is something satisfying in it. I have always thought that a man who knows there is more to be done, and is capable of doing it, is in a more fortunate position than a man who believes his work to be complete." I thought of Edmund Blackwood, ascending the stairs of the turret with his evidence secured and his purpose clear, the November night cold about him, the truth before him - and the thing waiting in the dark that he did not know was there. I thought of Miss Helena in the library with her notebook, writing the history of what had been done to her family with the same careful accuracy with which he had assembled his portfolio of documents - the same refusal to let dishonesty go unrecorded, carried forward by a different hand. I thought of Ashworth, patient and methodical and exact, following the thread of evidence wherever it leads, asking the question that needs to be asked, telling the truth that needs to be told. The fire burned low in the grate, and the July evening deepened toward dusk, and in the street below, London went about its ancient, multifarious, indifferent business. "Shall we dine?" I asked. "In a quarter of an hour," said Ashworth. "I want to re-read the Edinburgh letter first." I returned to my chair and my newspaper, and the evening settled around us in companionable quiet, and the work continued. It always continues. - Dr. James Pendleton, Montague Street, London, July 1893

"There is a distinction. Concern implies anxiety about an outcome one is powerless to affect. Interest implies engagement with a problem one believes to be soluble." "And you believe this problem is soluble." "I believe that any problem which has a factual answer is, in principle, soluble, given sufficient time and sufficient information. Whether the Edinburgh matter is solved in time to prevent further harm is a different question, and one that depends upon variables I cannot entirely control." He was quiet for a moment. "Edmund Blackwood attempted to solve it alone, without the resources that collective effort would have provided, under time pressure, with an adversary who was willing to use violence. We have the advantage of all three corrections: we are not alone, we are not under immediate time pressure, and our adversary does not yet know we are looking." "The hunter's advantage," I said. "Just so." He rose and moved to the window, and I saw him, as I had seen him on so many evenings, against the urban sky - that long, angular figure with its quality of unspent energy, its permanent alertness. "There is something satisfying," I said, "about a case that is not truly ended. About the sense that there is always more to be done." "There is always more to be done," he agreed, without turning from the window. "That is simultaneously the most discouraging and the most energising fact about this work. The cases end, but the problems they illuminate do not. Deception is not a temporary condition of human society; it is a permanent feature of it, and the response to it must be permanent, methodical, and unyielding."

Chapter II The Master of Blackwood Hall       Sir Percival Blackwood received us in the library of the Hall, a long, oak-panelled room lined with bookshelves of considerable antiquity, whose contents - calf-bound volumes of theology, natural history, and the law, interspersed with more modern works of a scientific character - suggested several generations of educated and moderately cultivated proprietors. A fire of notable generosity burned in the great stone fireplace, and the afternoon light, such as it was, filtered through tall windows whose leaded panes cast complex shadows across the Turkey carpet. The baronet was a man of perhaps fifty-five, of medium height and compact, well-muscled build, with a face that might have been handsome in youth but had been weathered by years of outdoor life into something more rugged and more interesting. His hair was iron-grey, his eyes of a deep and steady blue, and his bearing retained the upright precision of a man who had served in the military - I noted, indeed, the slight stiffness in his right shoulder which I associated, from my own experience, with the residual effects of a bullet wound. He came forward to greet us with a firm handshake and a directness of manner which I found immediately sympathetic. "Mr. Ashworth, Dr. Pendleton - I am most grateful to you for coming so promptly," he said, and there was in his voice a quality of controlled relief which told me that the composure he presented had been maintained at some cost. "Please be seated. I have ordered tea, for I imagine the journey from London has been cold." "Thank you," said Ashworth, taking the chair which faced the fireplace and settling into it with the alert ease of a man who is simultaneously resting and attending to everything around him.

Since his death, there have been occurrences within the Hall of a most disturbing nature - sounds without source, objects displaced from their stations, and, most troublingly of all, the appearance upon two separate occasions of a figure in the grounds which several members of my household staff have independently described as bearing the unmistakable resemblance of my late brother." "I do not submit to superstition, Mr. Ashworth. I am a man of rational temperament and scientific education. But I confess that these events have so thoroughly unsettled my household, and so persistently disturbed my own peace of mind, that I find myself wholly incapable of applying my usual faculties of reason to the problem. I would consider it a very great kindness if you would consent to visit Blackwood Hall at your earliest convenience. I remain, with the utmost respect, your obedient servant, Sir Percival Blackwood, Bart." "Well," said I, returning the letter to Ashworth, "the matter would appear to present some features of interest. A suspicious death, a ghost, and a baronet in distress - the ingredients of a most sensational narrative." "The ingredients," replied Ashworth, with that faint smile which I had learned to interpret as the outward sign of an inward enthusiasm carefully held in check, "of something considerably more substantial than a sensational narrative, if I am not very much mistaken. You will observe, Pendleton, that Sir Percival employs the word conscience when he speaks of his inability to accept the verdict of accident. It is a curious and suggestive choice of language for a man describing his intellectual misgivings." "You think he suspects foul play?"

"He did not specify the nature of this secret?" "He did not. And I - I confess to my shame, Ashworth - I was not particularly disposed to listen with sympathy. Our conversation did not end well. He left the room in a state of considerable agitation, and those were the last words I ever exchanged with him." Sir Percival turned from the window, and his expression was that of a man confronting a burden which is all the heavier for being self-imposed. "Three days later, he was dead." "I see. And the secret he mentioned - have you formed any hypothesis as to its nature?" "None that I am prepared to state with confidence. I have turned the matter over continually in my mind since his death, but Edmund was involved in a number of enterprises and associations of which I had only partial knowledge." "Very well. Let us turn to the events of the night itself. Who was present in the Hall on the evening your brother died?" Sir Percival returned to his chair and accepted the cup of tea which had been brought in by a stout, silent butler of advanced age, whom he addressed as Merton. He composed his recollections with visible effort. "The family party consisted of myself, Edmund, my daughter Miss Helena Blackwood, who is twenty-three, and my cousin Mr. Francis Ardley, who was then staying at the Hall. In addition, we had a guest from the village - Dr. Silas Moorgate, our local physician, who had dined with us and remained for the evening. The household staff were, of course, in their various quarters or engaged in their duties."

Sir Percival recovered approximately forty percent of his investment, which was, his solicitor told him, better than the average. For Sir Percival himself, the outcome of the investigation produced changes of a deeper kind than the merely financial. The revelation of Ardley's systematic exploitation of his family's trust was, initially, a wound of considerable severity - aggravated, I think, by the consciousness that his own distraction and preoccupation in the years preceding Edmund's death had created the conditions in which both the fraud and the estrangement from his brother could flourish. He was a man capable of honest self-examination, however, and such an examination, though painful, is generally more productive than its avoidance. In the months following the trial, he threw himself into the affairs of his estate with a renewed energy which its tenants and servants found at first surprising and then, as they experienced its practical effects, genuinely welcome. He restored the Hall's maintenance to a state of which Edmund, who had loved the old place with a proprietorial devotion, would have approved. And he rebuilt, with all the care and patience of a man who knows the value of what he is constructing, his relationship with his daughter. Miss Helena had been, in the years of his preoccupation, simultaneously his most reliable support and his most neglected responsibility - a combination which, in a person of lesser character, might have produced bitterness, but which in her had produced instead that quality of self-sufficiency combined with continuing affection which I found one of the most admirable of human achievements. Ashworth and I visited Blackwood Hall on several subsequent occasions over the following years - it had become, in the way of places associated with significant events in one's experience, a location invested with a particular quality of memory and meaning which made its company always agreeable. On one such visit, in the following autumn, I found Miss Helena in the library with a notebook before her, engaged in what appeared to be serious and systematic work. "I am writing a history of the Selkirk affair," she said, when I expressed my curiosity. "Not a sensational account - though there is sensation enough in the facts - but a careful and documented analysis of how such a fraud is constructed, how it exploits the social structures and personal relationships of its victims, and what lessons might be drawn for the protection of future investors. I intend to publish it under my own name." "That is an admirable undertaking," I said, and meant it. "Uncle Edmund would have done it himself," she replied, simply. "Since he cannot, I shall do it for him." There was a quality in her voice as she said this - the quiet, complete authority of a person who has arrived at a clear purpose and is entirely equal to its pursuit - which struck me, as I walked away, as one of the more remarkable things I had encountered in a year of remarkable encounters.

"The lever," I said. "Precisely. Ardley's plan, as I reconstruct it, was this: Edmund had ascended to the turret - I believe he was induced to go there by Ardley, though under what pretext I am not yet certain. Once Edmund was on the gallery, Ardley, from a position on the main roof below the level of the parapet, used the rope and lever to apply force to the exterior of the parapet at a point precisely calculated to throw Edmund off balance and over the battlements. The height of the parapet from the gallery floor would not prevent such an outcome if the force were applied at the right point and angle, and if the victim were taken sufficiently by surprise." The room was silent for a long moment, save for the fire's subsiding crackle. "And you believe," said Sir Percival at last, "that you have enough evidence to prove this?" "I believe that when Ardley's rooms are searched - which Inspector Graves is in the process of arranging - the missing portfolio of documents, along with certain other materials, will be found. The portfolio alone establishes the motive with precision and detail. Young William Briggs's testimony, combined with the physical evidence from the turret, establishes the means and the opportunity. Calder will, I suspect, under appropriate pressure, provide additional testimony regarding the arrangements between himself and Ardley." He paused, and something in his expression softened marginally. "The case is complete, Sir Percival. Not yet proven in a court of law - that is the work of the prosecution and the jury - but complete in its essential logic." "You are certain?" "In twenty years of this work," said Ashworth, "I have not yet arrived at a conclusion of which I was certain and subsequently found it false. I will not claim infallibility - it would be a foolish claim. But I am as confident in this case as I have been in any." He rose from his chair and moved to the window, where the first pale suggestion of dawn was beginning to dilute the dark. "Edmund Blackwood died because he was an honest man who would not be silent in the face of dishonesty. That is not, perhaps, a comfortable conclusion for those who loved him. But it is, if I may say so, an honourable one." "Thank you," said Miss Helena Blackwood, very quietly, and in those two words I heard the release of a contained and weighted breath that had been held for three weeks.

Sir Percival's face, during these narrations, had passed through several distinct phases of expression - from the controlled attentiveness of a man hearing an account he had half expected, through a barely suppressed anger, to something which I can only describe as profound, complicated sorrow. "He was my cousin," he said, very quietly. "He was," agreed Ashworth, with a gentleness unusual in him. "And he exploited that relationship with considerable deliberateness and over an extended period. I think you need not reproach yourself for having trusted him - trust, in such circumstances, is a virtue rather than a failing." "How was it done?" I asked. "The mechanics of Edmund's death - you said you had reconstructed them." "With some help from the physical evidence of the turret, and from the testimony of young William Briggs - the coachman's son, who was twelve years of age and who had the habit of sleeping in the hay loft above the stables, from which the east face of the Hall, including the turret gallery and a portion of the main roof, is visible on a clear night. He saw Ardley on the roof that evening, though he thought nothing of it at the time and did not mention it to the constabulary because nobody thought to ask him." "A child witness," I said. "A child witness of entirely credible character, who was sleeping where he ought not to have been and who consequently saw what he ought not to have seen. His testimony places Ardley on the main roof at approximately ten forty-five, with a length of rope and an object which young William described as a long iron bar."

"How did he come to suspect the venture?" asked Sir Percival. "Partly through a careful reading of the prospectus itself - Edmund was a man of mathematical ability and logical temperament, and he identified certain inconsistencies in the geological data. He then engaged, at his own expense, an independent geologist, a Dr. Pemberton of Edinburgh, who travelled to British Columbia and conducted an examination of the Selkirk properties. Pemberton's report was unambiguous: the mineral rights were largely worthless, the geological surveys in the prospectus were fraudulent, and the company was, at its foundation, a mechanism for the extraction of investor capital." "Edmund received this report how long before his death?" I asked. "Approximately six weeks before. He spent those six weeks assembling his evidence - the geologist's report, the documents regarding Ardley's commission agreement, the photographs which I found in his desk - and consulting the solicitor Hartley, whose advice he was in the process of following when he was killed. He had, I believe, two objectives: to secure the legal exposure of the fraud, and to recover, on behalf of himself and others, the money which had been invested on the basis of false representations." "And Ardley knew he was being investigated?" said Miss Helena. "Ardley suspected it. He had been watching Edmund's movements, and he knew about the engagement of the Edinburgh geologist, though he did not know the content of Pemberton's report. When Edmund approached Sir Percival with his secret - three days before his death - Ardley drew the correct inference that the evidence was substantially complete and that action was necessary. He could not allow Edmund to reach his solicitor with that portfolio."

"Several things of interest, and several notable absences of things which ought to be present. The inquest into Edmund Blackwood's death was concluded with remarkable expedition - three days after the event, and a verdict of accidental death returned by a jury of twelve men who appear, from the newspaper account, to have been thoroughly satisfied by the medical evidence. No witnesses were called who could speak to the specific circumstances of Mr. Edmund's presence in the turret. The inference drawn by the coroner was simply that he had ascended for reasons of his own, lost his footing, and fallen." "Is that not a reasonable inference?" "It is a possible inference. Whether it is reasonable depends upon a number of facts which the inquest appears not to have troubled itself to establish. Edmund Blackwood was forty-seven years of age and in good health, according to the certificate of his personal physician. He had no history of vertigo or of the nocturnal wanderings which might lead a man to ascend a turret at eleven o'clock at night, which was the estimated time of his death. He had dined well, drunk moderately, and spent the evening in apparently normal fashion. At some point between ten o'clock and midnight, he ascended to the turret gallery and fell. Nobody saw him go, and nobody heard him fall." "You say the inquest was conducted with expedition. Is that not common in cases where the verdict appears obvious?" "Common, certainly. But here the expedition is noteworthy for another reason. Sir Percival Blackwood is, as I have ascertained by certain inquiries of my own, the primary beneficiary of his brother's estate and of a very substantial life insurance policy which Edmund had taken out five years ago.

A pause that was a shade too brief to be natural. "Edmund and I discussed many things. I cannot recall whether this particular matter arose." "He never expressed any concern to you about your business activities?" "Edmund was not - always - the most charitable in his judgements of others' affairs. He was a man of strong opinions." "Quite so. Mr. Ardley, on the evening of your cousin's death, you remained in the drawing room until approximately eleven o'clock, at which time you retired. Is that correct?" "It is." "And you did not, at any time during that evening, ascend to the upper floor of the Hall?" "I did not." "You are certain?" "Perfectly certain." Ashworth regarded him for a moment with a gaze of patient, unwavering attention. "You mentioned that you have hopes of improving your financial situation. I understand that Edmund Blackwood held, among his personal investments, a substantial interest in a mining concern in British Columbia - the Selkirk Mining Company. Are you familiar with that company?" Something happened in Ardley's eyes - something swift and involuntary and immediately suppressed. "I believe I have heard the name." "It is, in fact, the company in which you yourself have recently acquired an interest through an intermediary - one Mr. Vincent Calder of Bishopsgate. Is that not so?" The silence that followed was longer and of a different quality. When Ardley spoke, his voice was still controlled, but the effortless ease had gone from it. "You have been making inquiries into my affairs, Mr. Ashworth." "I have been making inquiries into the death of Edmund Blackwood," replied Ashworth equably.

I sat therefore in the leather armchair by his fire, as I have sat on so many similar afternoons over so many years, and read the evening papers while he worked, and reflected, in the idle manner that such afternoons encourage, upon the thread of connection that runs through the various adventures of our acquaintance. There was a letter on his writing table - I caught a glimpse of the address as I passed - from the Edinburgh solicitors we had identified in the course of our research into the friend in the north. The investigation continues, in its quiet, methodical way. These things take time. At some point in the late afternoon, Ashworth concluded his experiment, cleaned his hands with the practised economy of movement that characterises everything he does, and settled into the opposite chair with a pipe and that expression of unwound attention which means, in him, not inaction but a different quality of active thought. "The Selkirk case," he said, after a while, as though continuing a conversation that had been briefly interrupted rather than one that had not yet begun. "I have been thinking about it." "In what connection?" "In connection with what we do and do not know. The principal actors have been dealt with. The fraud has been exposed. The immediate victims have been compensated, in part. But the original architect - our friend in the north - remains at large, and with him the possibility that some future variant of the same scheme will be visited upon different victims." "You are concerned about it." "I am interested in it." He drew on his pipe.

The surface of the gallery is smooth and even - there are no irregularities which might cause a man to lose his footing unexpectedly." He paused at a point which I gathered, from the faint discolouration of the stonework below the parapet, was the location from which Edmund Blackwood had fallen. "Here." He knelt upon the flags and examined the parapet and the immediate surrounding area with a lens which he produced from his breast pocket. I watched him work with the focused, economical attention to which I had grown accustomed - his examination was neither hurried nor prolonged beyond what was necessary, but covered every square inch of the relevant surface with thoroughness. "Well?" I said, when he had risen and was looking out over the parapet at the grounds below - a drop of, I estimated, some fifty feet to the frost-hardened earth. "Several things are of significance. First, there is no evidence of a struggle upon this gallery - no disturbed dust, no displaced grit, no marks upon the parapet consistent with a man grasping it in desperation. If Edmund Blackwood fell accidentally, he fell with remarkable tidiness. Second, there are here" - he indicated a point on the outer face of the parapet - "certain marks upon the stonework which I find very interesting indeed. Do you see? Three parallel horizontal scratches, made by some metal implement drawn across the stone in a direction consistent with someone applying a lever to this point from outside." I bent to look. The marks were faint but unmistakable once pointed out - three thin horizontal lines in the stone surface, at a height of roughly eighteen inches from the gallery floor.

Chapter I The Summons from Surrey       It was upon a raw and cheerless morning in the latter days of November, when the yellow fog lay thick and impenetrable over the rooftops of London and the gas-lamps burned with a pale and spectral luminosity, that my friend Mr. Cornelius Ashworth first brought to my attention the singular affair which I have since come to regard as one of the most perplexing and morally instructive investigations of his remarkable career. I had called upon him at his rooms in Montague Street, where he had resided since his return from an extended period of continental travel, and found him in that peculiar state of energised abstraction which invariably preceded some fresh engagement of his formidable intellect. Ashworth was standing at his window when I entered, his tall, angular figure silhouetted against the fog-diffused light, his hands clasped behind his back in that characteristic pose which I had come to associate with the deepest concentration. He was dressed in his customary fashion - a long black frock coat, immaculately pressed, and a silk cravat of deep burgundy - and he held in one hand a letter which he was evidently re-reading for the third or fourth time, judging by the creases at its fold. "Ah, Pendleton," he said, without turning from the window, "you have arrived precisely when I needed you most. Your timing, as ever, is either the product of extraordinary good fortune or of that instinctive sympathy which long acquaintance engenders between two individuals of compatible temperament." "Good morning to you too, Ashworth," I replied, settling myself into the leather armchair by the fire, which burned with welcome warmth against the November chill.

"Miss Blackwood's instinct is sound - an original architect of a fraud of this scope and sophistication is not a minor figure, and if he remains unidentified and unreachable by the law, then the justice achieved at the trial was partial at best." "You are drawn to the challenge," I said. "I am drawn to the truth, Pendleton. The challenge is its instrument, not its object." He opened the largest of the reference volumes and turned its pages with quick, precise movements. "I shall write to Miss Blackwood today, and I shall also consult certain colleagues in Edinburgh whose knowledge of commercial law and its practitioners in that city is considerably more detailed than my own. If we can establish the identity of our friend in the north, we will have completed what the original investigation left - not unfinished, exactly, but perhaps less finished than it ought to be." I agreed to assist, and we spent the better part of the following week in researches which I will not detail here, as they properly belong to a separate account. What I will say is that the direction of those researches pointed, by the end of the week, to a figure of some standing in Edinburgh's commercial world - a name which I will not yet commit to print, but which Ashworth pronounced, upon examining the relevant documents, with the precise and weighty emphasis he reserved for conclusions of which he was entirely confident. Whether that pursuit, and its outcome, will be the subject of a future narrative, I cannot yet say with certainty. Such things depend upon events, and upon the nature of the evidence which subsequent investigation uncovers, and upon whether my friend Ashworth - who is, of all the people I have ever known, the most genuinely indifferent to whether the world knows what he has accomplished - can be persuaded that the story is worth the telling.

Ashworth addressed each hypothesis with patient, methodical precision, explaining in terms accessible to the jury why each alternative was inconsistent with the totality of the evidence, and doing so without the defensive irritation which such challenges typically provoke in expert witnesses. At the end of the cross-examination, the barrister sat down with the expression of a man who has employed his best technique and found it insufficient, and the judge - a man of scientific education who understood the language of evidence - permitted himself a brief nod in Ashworth's direction which conveyed unmistakably the authority's opinion of the witness's credibility. The jury retired for three hours and returned verdicts of guilty on all counts. Ardley received the death sentence, subsequently commuted to penal servitude for life on grounds which were not made public but which Ashworth, who had certain connections in the relevant quarters, attributed to the intervention of persons of influence whose own connection to the Selkirk Company they were anxious to keep from full public scrutiny. It was an imperfect outcome, as justice frequently is, but it was justice nonetheless. Calder received a sentence of fourteen years, of which he served eight before dying in prison of the consequences of a constitution undermined by decades of anxious calculation. The financial aftermath of the Selkirk affair was resolved through a complex series of legal proceedings, overseen by the court and administered by receivers appointed for the purpose. A portion of the invested funds was recovered from Calder's assets, and a further portion - considerably smaller than the investors might have hoped - from the sale of the Selkirk mineral rights at their actual, as opposed to their represented, value.

Ashworth's reconstruction of the conspiracy was achieved not merely by accumulating facts but by constructing, from those facts, a coherent account of human motivation. This is, I think, a skill which is less commonly recognised as central to criminal investigation than it ought to be, and which the popular conception of detection - as a process of following clues to a hiding place - systematically undervalues. I have also reflected, in the weeks since the trial's conclusion, upon the role that luck plays in such investigations, and upon Ashworth's attitude toward it. He is not, I think, a man who credits luck very highly - he would argue, and has argued, that what appears as luck is generally the product of thoroughness applied in advance of necessity. The existence of young William Briggs as a witness looks, on the surface, like precisely the kind of lucky circumstance upon which an investigation may unpredictably depend. But Ashworth pointed out to me, when I made this observation, that Briggs had only become a relevant witness because Ashworth had made a point of speaking with every person on the estate who might conceivably have observed anything of relevance - including the children of staff members, including those who worked unusual hours. The luck of finding Briggs was the product of a systematic determination to leave no potential source of information unexamined. That determination is not luck; it is method. The case had, finally, a quality which I find in the best of Ashworth's investigations - those which illuminate most fully the human reality behind the criminal event. The murder of Edmund Blackwood was a tragedy in a specific and classical sense: a good man was destroyed by the very qualities which made him good - his honesty, his thoroughness, his refusal to let wrong lie unexamined - and the instrument of his destruction was a man whose intelligence and social ease were equal gifts applied to fundamentally ignoble ends.

He rose from the table with the sudden decision of a man who has completed one phase of thought and is ready to begin the next. You will accompany me." "With the greatest pleasure," I said, and meant it. Calder's offices occupied the first floor of a modest but well-appointed building in Bishopsgate - the kind of premises that conveyed commercial respectability without excessive display, which is precisely the impression most effective in attracting the type of investor who considers himself too shrewd for ostentation. A clerk of deferential manner and sharp eyes showed us into a waiting room of appropriate furnishings, and within five minutes, Calder himself appeared. He was exactly as the photograph had suggested - dark, sharp, watchful, and possessed of a manner of calculated ease which I found, like Ardley's, immediately suspect. He was, if anything, rather more impressive in person than in his image - a quality of physical authority in his bearing which the photograph had failed to capture. "Mr. Ashworth," he said, with perfect composure. "I am told you have called regarding a matter of some delicacy. I am afraid I have only a very few minutes, but please be seated." "I will be brief," said Ashworth pleasantly. "I am investigating the death of Mr. Edmund Blackwood, who was, as I understand it, a significant investor in your Selkirk Mining Company." The briefest pause. "That is correct. A most unfortunate tragedy. Mr.

These feelings were present, and they informed his sense of urgency and his determination to find the truth. They did not, however, distort his reading of the evidence or his assessment of the individuals involved. He was capable of recognising, for instance, that Sir Percival - for all his grief and his legitimate grievance - had not been wholly straightforward with the coroner, and he said so without varnishing the observation. He was capable of seeing in Ardley, alongside the calculating coldness of the murderer, a quality of intelligence and nerve which was genuinely impressive in itself. He was capable, in short, of perceiving human beings in their actual complexity rather than in the simplified moral categories which grief and outrage make available. This capacity, I have come to believe, is the central quality of his method - more fundamental than his knowledge of chemistry, more important than his powers of physical observation, more decisive than his ability to reason from particulars to generals. It is the capacity to see, and to see clearly, without the distorting mediation of what one wants or fears to see. The technical puzzle of the case - the mechanism by which Edmund Blackwood was murdered - was, in the event, the simpler element. Once the physical evidence of the turret had been properly read, the broad character of the crime was apparent. The harder work was the human reading - the understanding of who these people were, what they wanted, what they feared, and how those desires and fears had interacted with each other over time to produce the specific outcome of November the fourteenth.

"I understand," he said, "that you were very close to your uncle." "He was the best man I have ever known," she replied, simply and without sentimentality. That evening, at Ashworth's request, we arranged matters so that the Hall appeared to have retired early. Sir Percival was taken into our confidence and agreed, with visible relief at the prospect of action, to cooperate. By ten o'clock, the principal rooms were dark and the servants had been sent to their quarters. Only Ashworth, Sir Percival, and I remained awake, positioned in a triangle of observation which covered the most probable approaches to the haunted areas of the house. I confess that the hours of waiting, in the dark and cold corridor, with only the settling sounds of an old house for company, were not among the most comfortable of my experience. I had stationed myself in a window embrasure behind a heavy curtain, and from this position I could see perhaps forty feet of corridor in each direction, lit faintly by the moonlight through the windows at each end. It was at half past eleven that I heard the sound. It was not dramatic - not the theatrical creak of a door or the deliberate footfall of someone wishing to be heard. It was, rather, the sound of a careful, practiced movement - the almost inaudible whisper of a soft shoe on the corridor floor, and then, a moment later, the extremely faint sound of a key or pick-lock being applied to a lock.
